content:
Welcome back to another edition of ‘Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS’ from Scratch. This week, we continue evolving our framework with practices that make automation smarter, cleaner and more maintainable.
We begin by validating every record in our web table using a constructed JSON array, where each entry is stored as an object with key–value pairs. Instead of writing multiple test cases, we will demonstrate how a single dynamic test can iterate through all records, streamlining validation into a truly data‑driven workflow.
Next, we will move beyond JSON files and explore how to pass test data as fixtures, applying this approach to both our web table and file upload/download scenarios. This shift highlights how fixtures can simplify test management and improve reusability.
Then, we will dive into Debugging Made Smarter, showing how Playwright’s auto‑generated prompts can accelerate fixes and reduce friction during troubleshooting.
Finally, we will introduce the Playwright configuration file, covering foundational points that set the stage for deeper discussions in the coming weeks. This marks the beginning of a new series where we will continue to explore how config‑level settings (like screenshots, videos and traces) can transform your automation workflow.

What’s Inside This Week’s Roundup:
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 31: Smarter Test Scripts by Iterating Web Table Records Dynamically
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 31: Smarter Test Scripts by Iterating Web Table Records Dynamically | by Suresh Madhusanka Rodrigo | AI & QA Nexus | Jul, 2026 | Medium
In our 30th edition, we restructured the test data by defining a person’s array in JSON, where each record is stored as an object with key–value pairs. That change gave us a cleaner, more scalable foundation for automation. Now, in this post, we will take that idea further by showing how to validate every record in a web table dynamically without writing separate test cases for each person.
Traditionally, testers often create individual test cases for each row in a table. While this works, it quickly becomes repetitive and harder to maintain as the dataset grows. Instead, by leveraging the persons array, we can design a single reusable test that iterates through all records and validates their column values against the expected data.

• Defining Column Indexes in JSON: How to extend the test data file by adding indexes for all key columns in the web table, ensuring flexible and reusable validations.

• Iterating Through Records in the Test Script: Using a loop to dynamically process every record in the array, eliminating repetitive test cases and improving scalability.

• Dynamic Test Names with Template Literals: Generating unique test names at runtime by embedding variables, making results easier to trace and debug.

• Filling the Test Body with Validations: Combining JSON properties with reusable page object methods to validate all columns for each record, while keeping scripts maintainable and future‑proof.
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 32: Turning Test Data into Playwright Fixtures
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 32: Turning Test Data into Playwright Fixtures | by Suresh Madhusanka Rodrigo | AI & QA Nexus | Jul, 2026 | Medium
In this edition, we will look at another powerful way to externalize datasets. Instead of relying on JSON files, we will explore how to pass test data as a fixture. This approach opens up new flexibility in managing test inputs, making your automation framework even more robust and scalable.
In this blog we will be looking into the below points:

• Creating a JavaScript Fixture File: How to set up a dedicated TestBase.js file inside the util folder to hold your reusable dataset.

• JSON vs JavaScript Fixtures: Understanding the subtle but important differences in how keys are defined and how data can be structured in JSON compared to JavaScript.

• Understanding test.extend: A deep dive into Playwright’s test.extend method and how it allows us to attach custom fixtures to the test lifecycle.

• Exporting a Customized Test Function: Why we assign our extended test function to exports.test and how this makes it reusable across multiple test files.

• Importing the Fixture into a Test File: How to bring the fixture into your test scripts, alongside expect and your page object classes, so your tests can consume the dataset seamlessly.
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 33: Building a Smarter Test Base by Custom Naming and File Upload Automation
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 33: Building a Smarter Test Base by Custom Naming and File Upload Automation | by Suresh Madhusanka Rodrigo | AI & QA Nexus | Jul, 2026 | Medium
In the 32nd edition of this series, it demonstrated how to create a fixture for our test data related to the Web Tables page and how to use that fixture data effectively inside automation scripts. That laid the foundation for building cleaner, more modular Playwright tests.
In this 33rd edition of the series, it will extend that knowledge further by focusing on two key areas:

• Custom naming for extended test: Showing how we can give a different name (like testBase) instead of always using the standardized test.

• Extending fixtures for file upload/download: Defining new fixture data for file upload scenarios and writing scripts that consume this data to validate upload and download functionality.
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 34: Debugging Made Smarter by Harnessing Playwright’s Auto‑Generated Prompts for Faster Fixes
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 34: Debugging Made Smarter by Harnessing Playwright’s Auto‑Generated Prompts for Faster Fixes | by Suresh Madhusanka Rodrigo | AI & QA Nexus | Jul, 2026 | Medium
In this edition, we explore a smarter way to debug Playwright test failures. Instead of manually crafting prompts to feed into an LLM, we will see how Playwright itself can generate structured diagnostic output that doubles as ready‑to‑use prompts. This approach saves time, reduces human error and accelerates the debugging cycle.

• Introduction to Smarter Debugging: Why relying on Playwright’s auto‑generated context is more efficient than writing prompts yourself.

• Demo Setup with Intentional Failures: Creating failing test cases on purpose to showcase how Playwright surfaces rich diagnostic data.

• Running the Suite in UI Mode: Executing tests interactively to visualize failures and capture detailed logs.

• Copying Diagnostic Prompts: How to extract Playwright’s structured failure output and reuse it directly as LLM input.

• Leveraging LLMs for Instant Debugging: Feeding those prompts into an LLM to quickly identify root causes and actionable fixes.
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 35: The Power of Playwright Settings by Streamlining Your Testing Workflow with Ease
Playwright Chronicles: Crafting Elegant Automation in JS/TS from Scratch — Part 35: The Power of Playwright Settings by Streamlining Your Testing Workflow with Ease | by Suresh Madhusanka Rodrigo | AI & QA Nexus | Jul, 2026 | Medium
In this blog post, it will explore how the Playwright configuration file serves as the backbone of efficient test automation. It will be covering the below points.

• Introduction to the configuration file: Understand the role of Playwright’s configuration file as the central hub for defining test behavior, browser settings and reporting options.

• Locating the file in your project: Learn where this file resides in a typical project structure and why keeping it at the root ensures consistency and easy access.

• Creating a separate configuration: Discover how to set up alternate configuration files for special scenarios, allowing you to test under different conditions without touching the default setup.

• Executing tests with a custom config: See how to run tests using your newly defined configuration file, switching seamlessly between environments with simple command‑line options.

• Defining multiple projects in one file: Explore the projects array, where you can define multiple browser setups (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) within a single configuration file for scalable cross‑browser testing.

• Executing tests across multiple projects: Understand how Playwright multiplies your test coverage by running each test against all configured projects, ensuring broad validation with minimal effort.
